coredata.dc:title: TiO2/Carbon allotrope nanohybrids for supercapacitor application with theoretical insights from density functional theory

coredata.dc:description: TiO2/rGO and TiO2/MWCNT electrode materials have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The synergetic effect of TiO2/rGO electrode enhances the specific capacitance to a value of 558F/g at 1 A/g and having cyclic stability of 100% for up to 5000 cycles. The fabricated symmetrical supercapacitor device shows the maximum energy density of 14 W h/kg and power density of 9.6 kW/kg at 0.5 A/g and 32 A/g current density, respectively. TiO2/rGO electrode shows excellent cyclic stability leading to its claim as the potential electrode in the energy storage devices. From density functional theory calculations, we observed an enhanced density of states close to Fermi level for TiO2/rGO structure and charge transfer from 2p orbital of rGO to Ti 3d orbital of TiO2 signifying the enhancement in conductivity of the hybrid structure. Furthermore, the computed quantum capacitance has been increased for TiO2/rGO electrode which qualitatively supports our experimental findings.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2/rGO and TiO2/MWCNT electrode materials have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The synergetic effect of TiO2/rGO electrode enhances the specific capacitance to a value of 558F/g at 1 A/g and having cyclic stability of 100% for up to 5000 cycles. The fabricated symmetrical supercapacitor device shows the maximum energy density of 14 W h/kg and power density of 9.6 kW/kg at 0.5 A/g and 32 A/g current density, respectively. TiO2/rGO electrode shows excellent cyclic stability leading to its claim as the potential electrode in the energy storage devices. From density functional theory calculations, we observed an enhanced density of states close to Fermi level for TiO2/rGO structure and charge transfer from 2p orbital of rGO to Ti 3d orbital of TiO2 signifying the enhancement in conductivity of the hybrid structure. Furthermore, the computed quantum capacitance has been increased for TiO2/rGO electrode which qualitatively supports our experimental findings.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy demand is the hottest topic of research in the twenty-first century. The world’s energy consumption is still satisfied by the fossil fuels such as coal, oil, and natural gas. It is reported that energy consumption from the oil can be able to supply no more than 40 years [1]. Hence, the development of clean, cost-effective, eco-friendly energy storage devices (ESDs) is needed. Solar cells, electrochemical cells, supercapacitors (SCs), and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are fulfilling the above requirements. Considering the availability of lithium resources on the earth's crust, LIBs are replaced by sodium ion batteries (SIBs). However, SIBs show lower energy capacity and cycle stability. Hence in recent years, the SCs have been recognized as one of the outstanding ESDs for various high-power applications. It offers numerous benefits of high-power long-life upkeep operation, fast charge–discharge, and environmentally friendly process [2,3]. Energy usage can be significantly reduced by using the SCs with renewable energy resources and also can increase the ESDs lifetime. SCs have manifested as ideal energy storage devices owing to their better power density and extensive cycle life. It can be divided into two classes in terms of charge storage mechanism such as electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) and pseudocapacitors. In EDLC’s charges are accumulated in the electrode/electrolyte interface whereas, in pseudocapacitor, the fast surface faradaic reactions are occurred [4]. Carbon-based materials exhibit EDLC behavior while conducting polymers and transition metal oxides show pseudocapacitive nature. EDLCs play a vital role in ESDs because of their excellent electronic conductivity, high specific surface area (SSA), adjustable porosity/morphology, and excellent stability [5,6]. Carbon materials have been employed as a high-performance SCs application and the specific capacitance (Csp) value obtained from the EDLCs is 10–100 folds lower than to the pseudocapacitor. Pseudocapacitor exhibited a higher Csp value due to its multiple redox states, higher electrical conductivity, and different surface morphology.

RuO2 is an excellent pseudocapacitance electrode material which exhibits the high specific capacitance in the range of 1400–2200F/g [7]. Eventhough it has an excellent capacitance, the commercialization is very difficult due to its drawbacks (high cost, toxicity). So the researchers focusing the other metal oxides. i.e., MnO2 [8], Co3O4 [9], V2O5 [10], and WO3 [11]. Compared with these metal oxides, TiO2 anatase is considered a promising electrode for supercapacitors owing to its high thermal stability, high work function (> 0.2 e V), wide bandgap (3.2 eV) and high working voltage. However, it suffers from poor conductivity, limited electrolyte ion accessibility and poor charge/discharge cycles. The hybridization of carbon allotropes with metal oxides will improve the conductivity and structural stability of the hybrid system. A two-dimensional (2D) graphene with sp2 bonded carbon networks has outstanding features such as excellent electron mobility (2x105 cm2 Vs−1), conductivity (1x106 S cm−1), high transmittance (97.9%, visible light), Young's modulus (1100 GPa), and large SSA (2630 m2 g−1). Therefore, it has been used as additive with TiO2 electrode for energy storage application [12–14].

Even though a lot of works reported on the TiO2/rGO hybrid electrode for supercapacitor application, there is deficient to explore the physical insights for experimental capacitance performance through electronic structure calculation, orbital interaction, and charge transference. We found some of the theoretical supported experimental works for graphene/metal oxide-based hybrid system for some other applications. Zhu et al. [27] have performed first principle simulation to explore the Li+ storage on the TiO2/graphene system. The estimated Li+ adsorption energy over the surface (−3.10 eV) and subsurface layer (−3.85 eV) of B-TiO2−x/G are relatively higher than that of those of pristine TiO2 surface (−3.35 eV) and surface layer (−4.33 eV). Further, the calculated diffusion barrier energy of the hybrid is lower than the pristine TiO2 which giving more validation for the enhancement of the Li+ storage. Zhu et al. [28] have also carried out the DFT simulations to provide evidence for the enhanced degradation efficiency (92%) of hydrogen treated dopamine bridged melamine-graphene/TiO2 (H-TiO2@MG-D) Nano-capsules toward gaseous formaldehyde. The simulated bandgap for perfect TiO2, TiO2@MG-D, and H-TiO2@MG-D are 2.149, 1.946, and 1.929 eV, respectively and are matched with the experimental (UV–Visible -DRS) which confirmed the superior charge separation efficiency of H-TiO2@MG-D. The DFT calculation was done by Das et al. [29] for the enhancement of the supercapacitor performance of the Mn3O4/graphene hybrid systems. The computed DOS shows that the energy states near the Fermi level are higher than the bare Mn3O4 which strongly evidences the conductivity of the hybrid gets increased. This is due to the charge transference from C 2p orbital to Mn 3d orbital. The computed quantum capacitance trend also nicely matches with the experimental. The energy storage performance of VO2-graphene has been investigated through both experimental and theoretical according to Kamila et al. [30]. From the DFT, it is claimed that the improved conductivity of the hybrid is due to orbital interactions of V 3d orbital and C 2p orbital. The estimated quantum capacitance of VO2-graphene is higher than the VO2, which supports the experimental results.

The shortage of DFT investigation encourages us to derive a physical insight of orbital hybridization, charge transfer and interaction of TiO2/rGO hybrid structure which will give the information about the charge storage performance of the electrode. In this work, we have achieved a better specific capacitance value (558 F/g at 1 A/g) which is due to the enhanced electron transfer through the bond formation from C 2p to Ti 3d. The bond formation can enhance the conductivity of electrode material results the better supercapcitance performance. In order to confirm the above statement, we have performed DFT simulations to examine the electronic structure and quantum capacitance of the hybrid system. The electron transfer from C 2p orbital to Ti 3d favors the conductivity enhancement of the hybrid through C-Ti bonding. The computed quantum capacitance also qualitatively supports the experimental capacitance values.

Synthesis procedures for the preparation of bare and nanohybrids of TiO2, rGO and rGO/TiO2, MWCNT/TiO2 have been provided in the supporting information. The characterization and instrumentation details for the synthesized nanomaterials are also given in the supplementary section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of working electrodes for the fabrication of supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para:
For the supercapacitor electrochemical studies, the working electrode was fabricated by the mixing of T0/rGO (or) T0/MWCNT nanohybrids materials (active material), super-P carbon (conducting carbon), and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, binder) in the weight ratio of 75:20:5, respectively. The above mixer was ground well-using mortar and pestle to obtain fine powder-based electrode materials. The electrode mixer was dispersed to make a slurry using N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) as a solvent. Then, this slurry was coated over flexible graphite paper substrate (~2 × 2 cm2) and it was placed in a vacuum oven at 90 °C for 12 h. The active materials loading in the fabricated electrode was optimized to ~ 2.5 mg/cm2 for better performance. For the half-cell measurement, the nanohybrids coated graphite paper, Hg/Hg2SO4 (MMS, 1 M H2SO4), and a slice of Pt foil was used as a working, reference, and counter electrode, respectively. The electrode material of bare GO, rGO, and bare TiO2 nanoparticles was also prepared similarly for the comparison studies. The electrochemical characterizations of the synthesized nanohybrids were carried out using a Biologic SP-300 modular grade potentiostat/galvanostat/FRA electrochemical workstation. The electrochemical property of prepared nanohybrids electrode was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis using 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. The CV and GCD experiments were carried out for all the electrodes in the potential range of −0.6 to 0.4 V (vs. MMS) at different scan rates and current densities, respectively. From the discharge profile, the specific capacitance of the electrode was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)

where Csp (F/g) is the specific capacitance of the electrode material, I (A) is the applied charge/discharge current, Δt (sec) is the discharge time, ΔV is the working potential and m is the active mass of the working electrode (g).

Also, the symmetric full cell was fabricated using the best nanohybrids electrode material (T0/rGO) from the half-cell measurement using 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. The total capacitance of the fabricated cell (Ct), the specific capacitance of the electrode (Csp), coulombic efficiency (η), energy density (E), and power density (P) of the fabricated symmetric cell was calculated from the Eqs. (2)–(6).

Where, Ct (F/g) is total specific capacitance, Csp is the specific capacitance of the prepared electrode material (F/g), I is the charging/discharging current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), M is the total mass of the active electrodes (g), ΔV is cell potential (V), E (Wh/kg) is the energy density and P (W/kg) is the power density. EIS experiments were performed with a bias of 5 mV in the frequency range of 10 MHz to 100 kHz and the data was analyzed using a Nyquist plot.

XRD pattern of ideal layered structure of graphite shows a peak at 26.5° (0 0 2) with inter-distance (d) of 0.34 nm (Fig. 1 ). For the oxidized graphite (GO), a less intense peak at 10.2° (0 0 1) and a strong peak at 26.2° (0 0 2) are observed. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups and the structural defects in sp3 leads to the shift in the lower angle side (~10.2°). XRD pattern of GO shows a broad peak of (0 0 2) plane and slightly shifts from 26.5 to 25.4° corresponding interlayer distance (d-spacing) of 0.35 nm. This proposed that the maximum of the oxygen functional groups is not formed onto the basal planes of GO but attached to the edges of graphite. These edgily grafted oxygen groups possibly will lead to an edge expansion in solid-state which is supported by the broad peak of (0 0 2) on GO. In general, for the preparation of GO by Hummers method, the temperature is maintained below 30 °C and in this case, oxidation occurs at both the basal and edge planes. The prepared GO was synthesized at 50 °C. This higher reactive temperature leads to the decomposition of oxidizers and so the further oxidation at the basal planes is suppressed. The carbon atoms in the edges are speedy responsive than those at the basal planes and thus the oxidation starts favorably at the edge planes. Wei et al. have prepared a similar edge-selectively oxidized GO at 60 °C. The XRD pattern of free TiO2 shows the anatase phase (JCPDS 21-1272). The sharp diffraction peaks of pristine TiO2 NPs indicate the small crystallite size. The average crystallite size calculated from Scherrer’s equation is 14 nm. For T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT nanohybrids, the diffraction lines are indexed to the anatase phase. [31,32]

FTIR spectrum of GO, rGO, and MWCNT shows ( Fig. S2 ) the peaks at 3432, 2912, 1633, 1520, 1391, and 1027 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH groups (3432 cm−1), C-H stretching vibrations (2912 cm−1), water bending and CC stretching (1633 cm−1), the skeletal vibration of the graphene sheets (1520 cm−1), COH stretching vibrations (1391 cm−1) and CO vibrations from epoxy groups (1027 cm−1), respectively. Also, the peaks observed at 2522, 2330, and 1185 cm−1 exclusively are attributed to GO which is attributed to the sp 2, sp 3 hybridized CH bond, and OCOH stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups respectively. It is reported that the epoxies along with hydroxyl groups are located in the basal plane and whereas, carboxyl groups were occupying the edges of the plane. From the FTIR spectra, GO demonstrates a greater number of edge selective oxidations of carboxylic groups than rGO. Pristine TiO2 (T0) shows the peaks at 3428, 1633, 661, and 498 cm−1 results by the stretching, bend vibrations of OH groups, Ti-O stretching and Ti-O-Ti bridging vibrations respectively. In addition to that T0/MWCNT shows C-H stretching vibration (2921 and 2853 cm−1) and T0/rGO shows C-OH stretching vibration (1386 cm−1) [33,34].

The micro- Raman spectra of T0, GO, rGO, T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT are shown in Fig. 2 . GO shows the two significant characteristics peak corresponding to G-band at 1587 cm−1 (E2g phonon vibration of carbon sp2 atoms) and D-band at 1357 cm−1 (A1g breathing mode of structural defects and disorders). For rGO, the G and D bands get shifted to 1586 and 1362 cm−1, respectively. The defects-related D band shows a large shift of 5 cm−1 for rGO compared to GO. The D band is hardly visible in the pristine graphite. It has been reported that ID/IG ≤ 0.1 for pristine graphite due to the very weak D band. In our case, GO illustrates the ID/IG is 0.43. This D band could be attributed to edge distortion by introducing chemical defects on the edges. The intensity ratio of (ID/IG) of rGO is calculated to be 0.54. Since ID/IG is proportional to the average size of the sp2 carbon domain the increased ID/IG ratio is ascribed to the existence of defects in rGO, indicating that more numbers of oxygen functional groups have been introduced onto the basal and edge planes during the synthesis. Raman spectrum of pure TiO2 (T0) shows the peaks at 145.8 cm−1 (E1g), 399 cm−1 (B1g), 512 cm−1 (A1g, B1g), and 643 cm−1 (E2g) modes indicating the anatase phase of TiO2. For the composite of T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT, the intense peak E1g is red shifted to 147 and 149 cm−1 respectively. This may be attributed to the presence of more oxygen functional groups (carboxyl groups) in the composites. The ID/IG ratio of T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT is 0.82 and 0.57 respectively. This indicates that the defects are high in T0/rGO compared to T0/MWCNT [35–38]. The phonon lifetime of T0/MWCNT decrease compared to the T0 and T0/rGO as shown in Table 2 .

Fig. 3 shows spectra of C 1s for GO, rGO, T0/rGO, and T0/MWCNT. The C 1s peak of GO is fitted into five peaks of 284.4, 285.9, 287.1, 288.3, and 290.9 eV corresponding to (CC) sp2 bonded carbon, (CC) sp3 bonded carbon, carbonyl bonds (CO), carboxyl group (OCO) and shakeup satellite peak due to π-π* transitions respectively. Epoxy and hydroxyl groups are wrapped on both the in-plane and out-plane while carboxyl groups are mainly attached on the out plane of the oxidized graphite. C 1s peak of rGO shows sp3 bonded carbon (CC) at 285.1 eV, epoxy groups (CO) at 286.6 eV, carbonyl groups (CO) at 287.8 eV, and carboxyl groups (OCO) at 289 eV. This suggests that the defects on both the edges and the basal planes in rGO but the absence of epoxy groups in GO represent the edge-selective carboxylation. Sp2 bonded (CC) in graphitic structured GO has the highest composition of 61%. A reduction from 16% to 14% of carboxyl /carbonyl groups is observed for GO to rGO respectively. After the hydrothermal treatment from GO to rGO process, some of the epoxy groups are presented in the basal plane of the carbon atom. This leads to the restriction of the formation of a highly conductive sp2 (CO) matrix in rGO. Based on these results, GO is closer to pristine graphite in terms of structure than rGO. Fig. S3 indicates the O 1s state of GO, rGO, T0/rGO, and T0/MWCNT which illustrates that the intensity of the oxygen functional groups is high in rGO than the GO. In the composites, O2– oxidation states confirm in TiO2 lattices. Fig. S4 confirms the Ti4+ in TiO2 lattices in the composites [39].

The room temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement was employed to investigate the valence states and occurrence of paramagnetic defects such as Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies. As indicated in Fig. 4 pure TiO2 NPs show a single distinct signal at g = 1.9962 which is mainly assigned to the Ti3+ center associated with the oxygen vacancies. This signal appears by the reduction of TiO2 in the presence of oxygen vacancies (VO). The excess electrons in TiO2 are localized at the Ti4+ site reducing into Ti3+ ions via capturing electrons. When TiO2 is hybrid with either MWCNT or rGO, the g value is obtained at 1.9984 (T0/MWCNT) and 1.9981 (T0/rGO) respectively. Interestingly, the intensity of the Ti3+ signal is enhanced due to VO defect centers. Comparing to T0/MWCNT, T0/rGO nanocomposite shows a highly intense Ti3+ signal indicating the hybridization of rGO favors the V0 F centers [40,41].

The photoluminescence spectra of GO ( Fig. 5
a), rGO (Fig. 5 b), MWCNT ( Fig. 5 c), T0 (Fig. 6
a), T0/rGO (Fig. 6 b), and T0/MWCNT (Fig. 6 c) are shown with the excitation wavelengths of 290, 310 and 330 nm. Similar emission has been observed for the MWCNT with varying excitation wavelengths. However, for GO and rGO it is shifted to 7 and 16 nm from 290 to 330 nm, respectively. Shang et al. have observed the excitation-dependent fluorescence of GO and it originates due to the transition between CC of non-oxidized sp2 carbon regions to the boundary oxidized functional groups of CO, CO, and OCOH [42]. Mei et al. have stated that the excitation-dependent blue shift of fluorescence with varying sp2 clusters size [43]. Du et al. have examined the excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence of GO due to the formation of excimers by varying the pH [44]. Zhang et al. have observed the redshift on graphene quantum dots with the varying excitation caused by the wide size distribution of quantum dots [45]. XPS and Raman analysis confirm the mixture of sp2 and sp3 matrix in GO and rGO which have epoxy, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on the edges and basal planes. Oxygen functional groups can induce the structural disorder in carbon atoms which are located on π –π* gap (sp2 sites). In this work, the modified Hummers method has been used to prepare to GO. The extreme oxidation condition of GO can induce isolated nano-sized sp2 clusters within the sp3 carbon–oxygen matrix. This distorted sp2 matrix could form graphitic sp2 clusters. The optical property of the structurally induced localized sp2 states can cause a broad emission band. Hence the observed broad blue band emission of 400–480 nm is due to the localization of electron-hole pairs in the isolated sp2 clusters within the carbon–oxygen sp3 matrix. The emission spectrum of MWCNT shows almost constant with varying excitations.

Fig. 6 a shows the emission spectra of T0 with the varying excitations (290, 310, and 330 nm). T0 and T0/MWCNT (Fig. 6 c) exhibited similar emissions observed at 388, 424, 464, and 527 nm. The emission observed at 388 and 424 nm are due to the near band-edge emission (NBE) and self-trapped excitons (STE) respectively and the remaining peaks at 464 and 527 nm are due to the surface related oxygen vacancies. For T0/rGO (Fig. 6 b) the NBE and STE get blue-shifted from 388 to 371 and from 424 to 414 nm respectively and the width of the band also gets decreased. The reduction of GO for the formation in T0/rGO nanohybrid usually formed many small-sized sp2 clusters. The deoxygenation causes the decreasing of disordered sp2 defect states in π-π* gap and increasing of sp2 clusters which exhibit blue band with a narrower bandwidth. However, by changing the excitation wavelength from 290 to 330 nm, the NBE is blue-shifted about 9 (379 nm), 5 (366 nm), and 8 (380 nm) for T0, T0/rGO, and T0/MWCNT respectively. As discussed before, the excitation-dependent shift is due to the different sizes of TiO2 NPs. The possible emission mechanism of rGO is sown in Fig. 7
[46–49].

The comparative emission spectrum of GO, rGO, and MWCNT is shown in Fig. 8
a with the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. Emission of MWCNT has been found to the residual impurity and passivated defects on the nanotube surface. However, compared to the GO and rGO, the width of the blue band is decreased for MWCNT due to the less sp2 island entity. In this way, the observed emission shift is due to the electron-hole recombination of different sized sp2 clusters. The emission spectra of T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT are shown in Fig. 8 b which reveals the blue shift of NBE of T0/rGO under the excitation of 310 nm due to the increasing of epoxy group in rGO.

Fig. 9
a illustrates the FESEM image of the pristine TiO2 NPs. The particles are evenly distributed and Fig. 9 b reveals the FESEM image of rGO which is observed in the sheet-like structure. Fig. 9 c shows the TEM image of T0/rGO composite. It shows the dense TiO2 NPs (10 nm) are decorated hardly on the surface of graphene sheets which is because of the low percentage of rGO loading in the T0/rGO hybrid. The oxygenated functional groups on the graphene surface act as anchoring sites for the TiO2 NPS which causes for the massive attachment of NPs on its surface. Moreover, the attachment of NPs is due to the TiOC bond formation with rGO at the defect sites. Initially, the TiO2 NPs have even distribution with the rGO sheets after that the remaining particles attaching around the defect sites which lead to the agglomeration [50]. Fig. 9 d displays a TEM image of T0/MWCNT. It is visibly observed the 1D nanostructure with a length of a few microns and a diameter of around 10 nm. A large number of TiO2 NPs are anchored on the surface of MWCNT.

Fig. 10
a and Fig. S5 show the CV curves of GO, rGO, T0, T0/rGO, and T0/MWCNT electrodes from −0.6 to 0.4 V (vs. MMS) at 100 mVs and different scan rates, respectively. The shape of the CV curve is symmetrical rectangular for GO, RGO, T0 whereas, T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT electrodes exhibited a slight hump during charging and discharging. The pseudocapacitance behavior of T0 begins from the reversible redox reaction involving the exchange of surface or subsurface intercalation and de-intercalation of protons and/or cations within the electrolyte (H2SO4). The projected possible redox mechanism is described based on the following equation [51].
(7)
( T i
O 2
)

⇔

H +
)
surface
In general, the presence of carbonyl or quinone group on the surface of carbon allotrope (rGO and MWCNT) can interact with the H+ ions of the H2SO4 electrolyte which undergoing redox reactions. In our case also the prepared T0/rGO (T0/rGOCO) and T0/MWCNT (T0/MWCNT- CO) nanohybrids exhibit the Faradaic redox reaction which is attributed to the carbonyl group [52,53]. The presence of the carbonyl group is evidenced by the XPS analysis and the redox reaction given by
(8)
The existence of the carbonyl group on the surface of rGO (T0/rGO) and MWCNT (T0/MWCNT) exhibit the oxidation and reduction peaks around 0.05 V and −0.2 V, respectively. Similarly, Oh et al. [54] have studied the electrochemical behavior of reduced graphene oxide for 1 M H2SO4 and the observed redox peaks (oxidation: ~0.05 V, reduction: ~ −0.2 V) from CV curves nicely matches with our results which strongly supports our present investigation.

Note that these redox peaks were gradually increased with the initial cycling; they reached a plateau after few cycles ( Fig. S6 ). Moreover, T0/rGO electrode exhibited a larger integral CV area than GO, rGO, T0, and T0/MWCNT which depicts that the greater capacitive behavior. It is obvious that when the TiO2 nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed over the rGO surface, the specific surface area and electro-active sites enhance, which efficiently diminishes the diffusion length of the H+ ion in the electrode surface. Also, the addition of rGO in the composite electrode delivers improved conductivity and excellent interfacial contact between graphene and TiO2 nanoparticles, which in turns the quick transportation of electrons even at a high scan rate. The CV curves of all the electrodes display nearly rectangular profiles even at higher scan rates (100 mV/s), suggesting that the electrodes possess high surface accessibility with good electrical conductivity leads an ideal supercapacitor behavior (Fig. 10 a).

Fig. 10 b and Fig. S7 display the GCD curves of the GO, rGO, T0, T0/rGO, and T0/MWCNT electrodes at a constant current density of 1 A/g and different current densities, respectively. All the GCD curves, the nonlinear pattern was appearing within the potential range, which confirms the existence of faradaic nature TiO2 nanoparticles. The calculated specific capacitance for T0/rGO, T0/MWCNT, T0, rGO and GO is 558, 363, 360, 348, and 236F/g at 1 A/g, respectively. The rate capability of all the electrodes at a different current density ranging from 1 to 20 A/g is shown in Fig. 10 c. The rate performance of T0/rGO, T0/MWCNT, T0, rGO and GO at a high current density of 20 A/g is 46, 46, 26, 42, and 23%, respectively. Among all, hybrid materials show good rate presentation which is due to the synergetic effect of the Faradaic and non-Faradaic electrodes. In general, that carbon allotropes exhibit the electrical double layer capacitive behavior which belongs to non-Faradaic. Metal oxides exhibit pseudocapacitive behavior through reversible Faradaic redox reactions. The contribution of Faradaic (diffusion controlled) and non-Faradaic (capacitive controlled) capacitance of composite materials (T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT) are estimated by Trasatti method of analysis ( Fig. S8
a-c). The specific capacitance as a function of ν-1/2 and inverse specific capacitance as a function of ν1/2 curves are shown in Fig. S8
a and b. Linear fit in the plot and extrapolate the fitting line to y-axis gives the capacitive controlled capacitance (Co) and subtraction of Co from total capacitance (CT) yields the maximum capacitance derived from the diffusion-controlled process (Ci). Bar diagram of specific capacitance of the T0/rGO and T0/MWCNT electrode materials with the contribution of the capacitive (purple) and diffusion (blue) are displayed in Fig. S8
c. From the analysis, T0/rGO delivered an 66.8% non-Faradaic capacitance and 33.2% capacity derived from Faradaic process. Similarly, T0/MWCNT shows a 66.1% from non-Faradaic capacity and 33.9% from Faradaic process. The significant combination of non-faradaic and faradaic process helps for the enhanced electrochemical activities for the charge storage process [55]. The decrement of specific capacitance while rising current density is caused by the constrained transfer of ions to the electrode surface. Also, T0/rGO shows a higher specific capacitance compared to T0/MWCNT which is because more numbers of oxygen functional groups were presented in the rGO surfaces as evidenced from Raman analysis. These oxygenated functional groups can enhance the additional pseudocapacitive behavior of the electrode results in higher capacitance. In addition to that, the composited TiO2 NPs perform as a spacer and avoid the restacking of graphene sheets which facilitates insertion of a greater number of electrolyte ions with the graphene interface which may also increase the overall performance.

In the direction of examining the usefulness of all electrodes as supercapacitors, the capacitance retention is calculated for 5000 charge/discharge cycles at a high current density of 20 A/g (Fig. 10 d). The specific capacitance of T0/rGO was gradually increased up to 5000 cycles with the specific capacitance retention of 107% while, T0/MWCNT, T0, and rGO delivered specific capacitance retention of 87, 84, and 90%, respectively. EIS analysis was performed to estimate the conductivity of the materials and the results were presented in the form of a Nyquist plot ( Fig. S9 ). The absence of semicircles was observed in the higher-frequency region for all electrodes revealing the low interfacial charge-transfer resistance. The straight line in the low-frequency region reveals the lower diffusion resistance and the quick charge transport. The Nyquist plot shows the low internal and charge transfer resistance for T0/rGO than those of other electrodes. Therefore, T0/rGO electrode shows higher specific capacitance and excellent capacitance retention due to the superior electronic conductivity and rapid charge carrying kinetics properties.

For the practical utilization of T0/rGO nanohybrid, the symmetrical cell was fabricated and the CV, GCD, and EIS studies were performed using a two-electrode system. Fig. 11
a displays the CV curves for the symmetric cell between 0 and 1.0 V at different scan rates from 5 to 50 mV/s. The CV curves show the typical rectangular shape with trace hump due to the combination of Faradaic and non-Faradaic nature of the hybrid electrodes. GCD curves of the fabricated symmetrical cell at different current densities are shown in Fig. 11 b. The calculated specific capacitance of the cell is 400, 324, 249, 192, 152, 120 and 100F/g for 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 and 32 A/g, respectively (Fig. 11 c). This value is significantly high for the other hybrid capacitors so far reported (Table 1). Besides, the fabricated cell delivered high specific capacitance retention of 84% for 25,000 continuous charge–discharge cycles at 32 A/g high current density (Fig. 11 d). For all the 25,000 cycles, the coulombic efficiency of the cell remains 99.5% which indicates the high reversibility of the electrode during the GCD process without any other side reactions. Fig. 11 e shows the Nyquist plot of the symmetric device. It shows the straight line in the high-frequency region which confirms the capacitive behavior of the electrode with very little solution resistance and interfacial charge transfer resistance.

Ragone plot is a good demonstration to estimate the performance of the device for practical application. The energy density (E) and power densities (P) of the symmetrical device were estimated from the charge–discharge curves and are displayed on the Ragone plot (Fig. 11 f). The maximum E was achieved as 14 W h/kg at the P of 253 W/kg at 0.5 A/g current density. A very high current density of 32 A/g, the fabricated cell delivered the maximum P of 9.6 kW/kg and retains their E of 2 W h/kg. Hence, the electrodes in the fabricated symmetric supercapacitor device will be the best electrode for the energy storage devices. Hence the increasing the capacitance is due to the (i) synergistic effect both from TiO2 and the rGO which facilitates the pseudocapacitive and EDLC behavior, (ii) the oxygenated functional groups on the surface of the rGO also helps to improve the pseudocapacitive behavior and conductivity of the hybrid electrode. The symmetric cell electrode performance of the material is given in the schematic diagram Fig. 12
.

To qualitatively support the experimental findings and to provide theoretical insight for the improved charge storage performance in TiO2/rGO, we have done quantum simulations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) technique.

The first principle computations were performed by Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) code with the execution of projector-augmented wave (PAW) pseudopotentials [56–59]. The structural optimization was continued until the residual force reaches to 0.01 eV/Ả. 500 eV plane wave cut-off energy was used for the extension of the electron wave function. TiO2 anatase bulk geometry with a tetragonal crystal system was taken for the electronic calculation. DFT + U approach was considered for accurate bandgap estimation. The Hubbard parameter U (=6) value was applied to the Ti 3d electrons. For bulk, TiO2 structure relaxation, 11x11x3 mesh, Monkhorst, and Pack was used [60]. The surface (1 0 1) was relaxed using 5x5x1 k-points. Grimme DFT-D2 dispersion correction was made for van der Waals force between the TiO2 surface and RGO [61].

The optimized geometry of bulk anatase TiO2 is shown in Fig. 13
a. The computed lattice constants are a = b = 3.820, c = 9.670 Å, matches nicely with the reported theoretical [62] value of a = b = 3.778, c = 9.549 Å. Our computed lattice parameters are also in good agreement with the published experimental [63] value of a = b = 3.785, c = 9.512 Å. Optimized Ti-O bond length (2.3 Å) almost matches with literature (2.0 Å) as reported by Fang et al. [64]. The total density of states (TDOS) of bulk anatase TiO2 computed with GGA (U = 0) and GGA + U (U = 6 eV) method are displayed in Fig. 14 a. The estimated band gaps are 1.87 and 2.98 eV for GGA and GGA + U method, respectively. The bandgap obtained from DFT + U approach well matches with the reported bandgap of 3.08 eV [62]. Reasonable matching of lattice parameters and electronic bandgap brings confidence regarding the accuracy of the simulation procedures and the methods. Fig. 14 b displays the partial density of states of Ti 3d and O 2p orbitals, reflecting that the conduction band is dominated by Ti 3d orbital while the valance band is dominated by O 2p orbital.

Since we are focusing on the electrode material, the rest of the calculations are on surface structure (slab geometry). From the relaxed bulk structure, we have generated a TiO2 (1 0 1) surface as this plane exhibit an intense XRD peak. Geometry optimized structure of TiO2 (1 0 1) is depicted in Fig. 13 b. Next, we built the hybrid structure of TiO2 + RGO. A reduced graphene oxide slab was constructed by attaching an epoxy group on the graphene layer and allowed it to relax. While making the hybrid structure of TiO2 + RGO, the lattice mismatch at the boundary of supercells issue also has in mind, to overcome this obstacle both cell dimensions have been taken more or less equal. TiO2 (1 0 1) surface having 72 atoms (Ti = 24, O = 48) with dimension of a = 11.46, b = 10.39 Å, whereas RGO layer containing 60 carbon atoms having dimension of a = 12.30, b = 12.78 Å was considered. The RGO layer was placed over the relaxed TiO2 (1 0 1) slab at different distances (2.3, 2.5 Å) and allowed to relax by incorporating Van der Waal interactions. The optimized structure of the binary hybrid layers TiO2 + RGO is given in Fig. 13 c. The detailed modeling of hybrid structure is displayed by a schematic diagram in Fig. S10 . Fig. 15
a display the TDOS for TiO2 (1 0 1) and TiO2 + rGO hybrid, compared to bare TiO2, the hybrid having well defined enhanced states near the Fermi level which indicates towards improved conductivity of the system.

Where, the components CQ and CEDL represent the quantum capacitance and electric double-layer capacitance, respectively. Also, in the above relation, the term CEDL depends on the interfacial interaction of electrode–electrolyte and CQ is substantial for nanomaterials. The computed quantum capacitance for RGO and TiO2 + RGO over the electrode potential is shown in Fig. 15 b. The hybrid of TiO2 + RGO exhibits the higher quantum capacitance (almost 4 times) as compared to the bare RGO surface. The interaction between RGO and TiO2 layer may involve both weak van der Waals interactions as well as chemical interactions. To get some insight regarding the charge transfer possibility between the orbitals of the hybrid structures, the PDOS of TiO2 + RGO compared with TiO2 surface and RGO layer is given in Fig. 16
a-c.

From Fig. 16 a, Ti d of TiO2 + RGO is highly populated compared to the TiO2 surface which confirms that some amount charge has been transferred from rGO. The O (of TiO2) 2p orbital energy states of hybrid structure also enhanced compared to TiO2 surface (Fig. 16 b). The C 2p of the hybrid structure having less intense states than the RGO layer evidencing the charge transferred to both Ti 3d and O 2p which enhances the conductivity of the hybrid system (Fig. 16 c). The Charge density plot for TiO2 + RGO with an isosurface value of 0.06e is shown in Fig. 17 . Hence, the charge transport property between TiO2 and RGO has been enhanced the capacitance value by the bond formation of C and Ti 3d. Our theoretical formulation supports the outstanding performance of the T0/rGO supercapacitor device.

T0/rGO electrode shows better supercapacitive performance compared to T0 due to the synergistic effect of the hybrid structure. To support the experimental observation, state-of-the-art DFT simulations have been carried out. The enhanced density of states around Fermi level for the hybrid structure and charge transfer from C 2p orbital to TiO2 indicates improved conductivity of the hybrid structure. Also, the quantum capacitance has been increased for TiO2 + RGO electrode which follows the same trend as observed in the experiment for specific capacitance.
